title: ONGC Geophysics — Full-length Mock 08
pattern: ONGC CBT — 85 MCQ · 120 min · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Solid-Earth Geophysics · Density and Velocity Structure
questions.stem: The Preliminary Reference Earth Model (PREM) provides one-dimensional radial variations of Earth's physical properties. Which of the following correctly describes the general trend of P-wave velocity with depth through the mantle?
options: P-wave velocity decreases monotonically from the surface to the core-mantle boundary
P-wave velocity generally increases with depth through the mantle, with a major drop at the outer core boundary
P-wave velocity remains approximately constant throughout the mantle
P-wave velocity increases only in the uppermost 400 km and then decreases
questions.solution: In the PREM, P-wave velocity generally increases with depth through the mantle due to increasing pressure and phase transitions. The velocity rises from ~8.1 km/s at the Moho to ~13.7 km/s at the base of the mantle. At the Gutenberg discontinuity (~2,900 km), P-wave velocity drops sharply from ~13.7 km/s to ~8.06 km/s as the waves enter the liquid outer core.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: At the center of the Earth, the pressure reaches approximately 360 GPa and the temperature is estimated at about 5500°C. The pressure at any depth is governed by dP/dz = ρg. Why is the pressure at the core-mantle boundary (~135 GPa) not simply proportional to the overlying mass alone?
options: Because gravitational acceleration g increases linearly with depth throughout the Earth
Because both density ρ and gravitational acceleration g vary with depth due to self-compression and mass redistribution
Because the outer core is liquid and exerts no overburden pressure
Because the Coriolis effect reduces the effective weight of overlying material
questions.solution: The pressure at depth depends on the integral P = ∫₀ᶻ ρ(z)g(z) dz. Both ρ(z) and g(z) vary significantly with depth. Density increases due to self-compression and phase transitions. Gravity g decreases slightly through the lower mantle because much of the Earth's mass is concentrated in the mantle. The interplay of increasing density and varying gravity makes the pressure profile non-linear.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The geotherm describes temperature variation with depth inside the Earth. In the upper mantle, the temperature gradient is steeper than in the lower mantle. Which physical mechanism primarily explains why the lower mantle has a relatively small temperature gradient despite its enormous thickness?
options: Radiative heat transfer dominates over conduction in the lower mantle
The lower mantle is a perfect conductor, so temperature is isothermal
Mantle convection efficiently transports heat, making the convecting mantle nearly adiabatic (~0.3–0.5 K/km)
The lower mantle contains no radioactive heat sources
questions.solution: The lower mantle convects as a nearly adiabatic system. In a vigorously convecting fluid, the temperature gradient approaches the adiabatic gradient (~0.3–0.5 K/km for the lower mantle), much smaller than the conductive gradient in the lithosphere (~10–30 K/km). The thermal boundary layers at the top and bottom of the convecting mantle have steep conductive gradients, but the interior is efficiently mixed by convection.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The geoid is defined as the equipotential surface of the Earth's gravity field that best fits mean sea level. The geoid-ellipsoid separation (geoid undulation N) arises because:
options: The Earth rotates on its axis, causing centrifugal bulging at the equator
The Earth's internal density is laterally heterogeneous, causing the gravity field to deviate from that of a uniform ellipsoid
Atmospheric pressure variations distort the gravity field
Ocean tides permanently elevate sea level above the reference ellipsoid
questions.solution: The geoid-ellipsoid separation (N) exists because the Earth's internal mass distribution is not laterally uniform. Density variations — such as subducted slabs, mantle plumes, continental roots, and oceanic trenches — cause the actual gravity field to deviate from the smooth, rotationally symmetric field of a reference ellipsoid. The geoid undulation can be positive (e.g., over India ~+20 m) or negative (e.g., over the Indian Ocean ~−100 m).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: NMR porosity is derived from the initial amplitude of the T2 decay curve, proportional to total hydrogen protons in pore fluid. Compared to density porosity, NMR porosity has the advantage of:
options: Being independent of the matrix mineralogy — it directly measures fluid content
Having higher vertical resolution than all other porosity logs
Being able to measure porosity in gas-filled formations without correction
Providing porosity measurements without requiring any calibration
questions.solution: NMR porosity measures the hydrogen index of the pore fluid. Since the measurement responds to the fluid itself rather than the matrix, NMR porosity is inherently mineralogy-independent. Density porosity requires knowledge of matrix density, and an incorrect lithology assumption leads to erroneous porosity.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In Differential GPS (DGPS) positioning, the base station computes corrections for satellite ephemeris, ionospheric delay, and tropospheric delay. The primary limitation of DGPS accuracy at long baselines (>50 km) is:
options: The receiver clock error, which cannot be removed by differencing
The spatial decorrelation of atmospheric (ionospheric and tropospheric) errors between base and rover
The number of visible satellites being reduced at long distances
Multipath errors at the base station being amplified at the rover
questions.solution: DGPS relies on the assumption that atmospheric errors are spatially correlated between base and rover. At long baselines, this assumption breaks down: the ionospheric and tropospheric paths diverge significantly, and the corrections computed at the base become less representative of the rover's errors. This is why RTK GPS is typically limited to baselines under 30–50 km for centimeter-level accuracy.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The eikonal equation |∇T(x)|² = 1/v²(x) relates the travel time field T(x) to the local velocity v(x) in the high-frequency approximation. In a region of constant velocity, what is the shape of the wavefronts described by the eikonal equation?
options: Ellipsoidal wavefronts centered on the source
Concentric spherical wavefronts expanding from the source
Planar wavefronts perpendicular to the propagation direction
Hyperbolic wavefronts focused at a caustic
questions.solution: In a constant-velocity medium, the eikonal equation reduces to |∇T|² = 1/v₀², which has the solution T(x) = |x − x₀|/v₀ — a family of concentric spheres centered at the source. The wavefronts are therefore spherical, and ray paths are straight lines emanating from the source.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In seismic ray theory, the ray parameter p = sin θ(z)/v(z) is constant along a ray in a 1-D velocity model. What physical condition corresponds to a ray with p = 0?
options: A ray traveling horizontally at the surface
A ray traveling vertically downward and upward (normal incidence)
A ray trapped in a low-velocity waveguide
A ray arriving at the critical angle of refraction
questions.solution: The ray parameter p = sin θ/v. When p = 0, sin θ = 0 (θ = 0°), meaning the ray travels straight down and straight back up. This is the normal-incidence ray, the ray path assumed in zero-offset seismic profiling. At the other extreme, p = 1/v₀ corresponds to a horizontally traveling ray at the surface.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Seismic tomography uses travel time residuals to image 3-D velocity heterogeneities. The relationship δt = −∫(δv/v²) ds is an inverse problem. What makes it non-unique?
options: Because the velocity model is known exactly and we only need to compute travel times
Because many different velocity distributions can produce the same set of travel time observations, and ray coverage is incomplete
Because the source locations are always known precisely
Because the number of seismic stations always exceeds the number of unknowns
questions.solution: Seismic tomography is non-unique because: (1) multiple velocity models can fit the same travel time data equally well, (2) ray coverage is uneven — regions with few rays have poor resolution, and (3) the problem is typically underdetermined. The solution quality depends critically on ray geometry — crossing rays from different sources and receivers provide the best constraints.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In EM theory, the Helmholtz equation (∇² + k²)ψ = 0 governs time-harmonic fields, where k = ω√(με − iμσ/ω). For a highly conductive medium (σ ≫ ωε), what does the complex wave number reduce to?
options: k ≈ ω√(με), representing a lossless dielectric
k ≈ (1+i)√(ωμσ/2), representing a strongly attenuating diffusive regime
k ≈ iω√(μσ), representing pure magnetic diffusion
k ≈ √(μσ), independent of frequency
questions.solution: When σ ≫ ωε (the quasi-static limit), the displacement current is negligible. The wave number becomes k ≈ √(−iωμσ) = (1+i)√(ωμσ/2). This represents the diffusive regime where EM fields decay exponentially with depth (skin depth δ = √(2/ωμσ)) and propagate with frequency-dependent phase velocity. This is the foundation of magnetotelluric and controlled-source EM methods.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Green's function for the Laplace equation in free space is G = 1/(4π|r−r′|). For a point source near a planar interface between two half-spaces with different conductivities, the method of images requires an image source. The strength of the image source relative to the primary source is:
options: Equal in magnitude but opposite in sign
Scaled by the reflection coefficient K = (σ₁−σ₂)/(σ₁+σ₂)
Always zero for electrostatic problems
Proportional to the ratio of the two conductivities (σ₂/σ₁)
questions.solution: In the method of images for a point current source near a planar conductivity interface, the image strength is scaled by the reflection coefficient K = (σ₁−σ₂)/(σ₁+σ₂). When σ₁ = σ₂, K = 0 (no image). When σ₂ = 0 (insulating boundary), K = 1. This image theory approach is widely used in DC resistivity, EM modeling, and self-potential interpretation.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In potential field theory, the boundary integral equation method solves Laplace's equation by transforming volume integrals into surface integrals. For a 3-D body with anomalous density contrast Δρ, what is the primary advantage of the BIE approach over finite-difference methods?
options: It requires discretization of the entire 3-D volume, providing higher accuracy
It only requires discretization of the body's surface, reducing the dimensionality of the problem
It can only be used for bodies with simple geometric shapes
It automatically satisfies the boundary conditions at infinity
questions.solution: The boundary integral equation method reduces a 3-D problem to a 2-D surface discretization using Green's third identity. Only the surface of the anomalous body needs to be meshed, dramatically reducing unknowns. The Green's function automatically satisfies the radiation condition at infinity, so no artificial boundary conditions are needed.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Potential & Time-Varying Fields · Laplace Equation in Layers
questions.stem: For a 1-D layered earth model, the potential field in each layer is expressed as a sum of upward- and downward-decaying exponentials. When matching boundary conditions across N interfaces, the resulting system is solved efficiently using:
options: Gaussian elimination on the full N×N matrix
A recursive algorithm (e.g., the propagator matrix or Kennett reflectivity method) that cascades interface conditions
Iterative relaxation methods requiring no matrix assembly
Monte Carlo random sampling of layer parameters
questions.solution: The propagator matrix provides an elegant recursive solution. Each layer's effect is represented by a 2×2 matrix that propagates field variables from one interface to the next. By cascading these matrices, boundary conditions at the surface and base are connected in O(N) operations rather than O(N³) for full matrix inversion.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In the U-238 decay series, secular equilibrium is reached when daughter activity equals parent activity. If the ²²⁶Ra/²³⁸U activity ratio deviates from 1.0 in a uranium ore body, what is the most likely geological reason?
options: The rock has not existed long enough for equilibrium to be established
Selective leaching or precipitation of radium (or radon emanation) has disturbed the equilibrium, indicating recent geochemical processes
The deviation is always due to analytical error in the measurements
The deviation is caused by cosmic ray interactions with the uranium series
questions.solution: Secular equilibrium should be achieved within ~10 half-lives of the longest-lived daughter (~16,000 years for ²²⁶Ra). A deviation from unity indicates recent geochemical fractionation: radium may have been preferentially dissolved by groundwater or radon (²²²Rn, a gas) may have escaped. Uranium-series disequilibrium is used in geochronology and environmental monitoring.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The equivalent stratum principle states that the gravity anomaly of any arbitrarily shaped 3-D body can be reproduced exactly by a thin layer with varying density placed on a surface. In gravity inversion, this principle is used to:
options: Eliminate the need for any forward modeling
Replace a complex 3-D density distribution with a surface density distribution, simplifying the inverse problem to finding surface mass density
Prove that all gravity anomalies are caused by surface masses
Calculate the Bouguer correction more accurately
questions.solution: The equivalent stratum method parameterizes inversion by placing a thin layer of unknown density contrast on a surface and solving for the surface density distribution that reproduces the observed anomaly. This reduces unknowns from a 3-D volume to a 2-D surface, making the inverse problem more tractable. It is exact mathematically but non-unique.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: For a buried spherical body, the half-width rule states that depth ≈ 1.3 × half-width of the gravity anomaly. If the half-width is 200 m, what is the approximate depth to the center?
questions.solution: Using the half-width rule: depth z ≈ 1.3 × half-width = 1.3 × 200 m = 260 m. For a sphere, the exact relationship is z ≈ 1.305 × half-width. Similar rules exist for other geometries: a vertical cylinder (z ≈ half-width), a horizontal cylinder (z ≈ half-width), and a vertical dike (z ≈ half-width for the derivative anomaly).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Koenigsberger ratio Q = Jr/(κ×He) compares remanent to induced magnetization. A rock with Q >> 1 is dominated by remanent magnetization. Which rock type typically has the highest Q?
options: Limestone with low magnetic mineral content
Basalt containing fine-grained titanomagnetite with stable single-domain grains
Quartz sandstone with trace iron oxide impurities
Deep-sea clay with dispersed iron sulfides
questions.solution: Basalts with fine-grained titanomagnetite single-domain (SD) grains typically have Q = 10–100 or more. SD grains are the most efficient carriers of remanent magnetization because they have a single magnetic domain with a large, stable magnetic moment. The induced magnetization of these grains is relatively small because SD grains cannot easily change their magnetization in response to the ambient field.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Reduction to the Pole (RTP) recalculates the total field anomaly as if measured at the magnetic pole (inclination = 90°). Why is RTP important for magnetic anomaly interpretation?
options: RTP removes the diurnal variation from the magnetic data
RTP centers the anomaly directly over its source, simplifying interpretation at non-polar latitudes
RTP converts total field data to vertical component data
RTP amplifies shallow anomalies while suppressing deep sources
questions.solution: At non-polar latitudes, the Earth's magnetic field is inclined, causing the total field anomaly to be asymmetric and its peak to be displaced from directly above the source. RTP transforms the anomaly to what it would look like at the magnetic pole, producing a symmetric anomaly centered over the source. This dramatically simplifies interpretation.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Magnetic Methods · Remanent vs Induced Magnetization
questions.stem: A rock sample has κ = 0.05 SI, Jr = 15 A/m, and He = 45 A/m. If Jr is antiparallel to κHe, what is the magnitude of the total magnetization and the Koenigsberger ratio?
questions.solution: Induced magnetization: Ji = κHe = 0.05 × 45 = 2.25 A/m. Since Jr is antiparallel to Ji: J = |Jr − Ji| = |15 − 2.25| = 12.75 A/m. Q = Jr/Ji = 15/2.25 = 6.67. Since Q > 1, the rock is dominated by remanent magnetization. In magnetic interpretation, failing to account for remanence can lead to incorrect source estimates.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Geophysical Inversion · Over-determined and Under-determined Problems
questions.stem: In geophysical inversion, an over-determined problem has more data than model parameters (N_data > N_params), while an under-determined problem has fewer data than parameters. For an under-determined problem, the inverse solution:
options: Is always unique because the system is overdetermined
Is non-unique — infinitely many models fit the data, and additional constraints (regularization) are required to select a preferred solution
Can be solved exactly using Gaussian elimination
Requires more data collection before any inversion can be attempted
questions.solution: In under-determined problems, the number of unknowns exceeds the number of independent observations, making the system rank-deficient. The null space of the forward operator contains non-zero vectors, meaning infinitely many model combinations produce the same data. Regularization (Tikhonov, minimum norm, smoothness constraints) selects a unique solution by imposing additional criteria such as model simplicity or closeness to a reference model.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Electrical Methods · Sounding Curves and Equivalence
questions.stem: In Vertical Electrical Sounding (VES), the equivalence principle states that different models can produce identical apparent resistivity curves. For a thin resistive layer (T-equivalence), what is well determined?
options: VES can always uniquely determine all layer parameters
A thin resistive layer cannot be uniquely resolved — only its transverse resistance T = ρ×h is well determined
The equivalence principle only applies to conductive layers
Adding more electrode spacings eliminates the equivalence problem
questions.solution: For T-equivalence, the VES curve is sensitive to the transverse resistance T = ρh but not to ρ and h individually. A thin, very resistive layer produces the same curve as a thicker, less resistive layer if T is the same. This non-uniqueness must be addressed by adding independent constraints from borehole data or other geophysical methods.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In a Schlumberger array for Vertical Electrical Sounding, the potential electrodes M and N are kept close together. What is the primary advantage over the Wenner array for depth sounding?
options: The Schlumberger array has a simpler field procedure
The Schlumberger array requires fewer electrode repositioning — only the outer current electrodes are moved for each reading
The Schlumberger array always provides a stronger signal
The Schlumberger array is immune to telluric noise
questions.solution: In the Schlumberger array, only the current electrodes (A and B) are moved outward between soundings while the potential electrodes remain fixed. This means fewer electrode moves per sounding — the crew only repositions 2 electrodes instead of 4. The fixed potential electrodes also provide a consistent reference for drift correction.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Electromagnetic Methods · In-phase and Quadrature Components
questions.stem: In FDEM methods, the received signal is decomposed into in-phase and quadrature components. For a highly conductive target at low induction number, the ratio of in-phase to quadrature response:
options: Approaches zero because the quadrature component always dominates
Is very large because the in-phase component dominates for good conductors
Equals exactly 1 for all conductors regardless of conductivity
Is frequency-independent and equals the conductivity contrast
questions.solution: For a conductive target in a FDEM system, at high induction numbers (good conductors or high frequencies), the in-phase component saturates at a maximum while the quadrature component peaks and then decreases. Therefore, for a highly conductive target, the in-phase/quadrature ratio is large. This ratio is used to distinguish good conductors (potential massive sulfides) from poor conductors (conductive clays).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The EM response of a thin conductive plate is characterized by α = σμωt². At what value of α does the quadrature response reach its maximum?
options: α ≈ 0.1 (very low induction)
α ≈ 1 (moderate induction)
α ≈ 10 (high induction)
α ≈ 100 (very high induction)
questions.solution: For a thin conductive plate in a FDEM system, the quadrature response peaks at α ≈ 1 (more precisely α = 2/√3 ≈ 1.15). At very low α, both components are small. At α ≈ 1, the quadrature reaches its maximum — the resonance condition where energy coupling is most efficient. At high α, the in-phase saturates and quadrature decreases as 1/α.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In a TDEM phasor diagram, after the transmitter current is turned off, the secondary field from a conductive body rotates from the quadrature axis toward the in-phase axis with time. This rotation indicates:
options: The body is becoming more resistive over time
The dominant frequency content of the decaying secondary field is shifting to lower frequencies (diffusion into the conductor)
The magnetic permeability of the body is increasing
The transmitter-receiver distance is decreasing
questions.solution: When the transmitter current is switched off, induced eddy currents start near the conductor surface and diffuse deeper over time. The frequency content shifts from high (early time) to low (late time). On the phasor diagram, this causes rotation from the quadrature axis toward the in-phase axis. The rate of rotation and decay is diagnostic of the conductor's size, conductivity, and depth.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: SEG-Y is the standard digital data format for seismic reflection data. What information is stored in the 3200-byte EBCDIC text file header at the beginning of a SEG-Y file?
options: The complete processing history of the data
Descriptive text about the survey (location, date, operator, client, and other survey metadata)
The navigation coordinates of all shot and receiver positions
The amplitude values of all seismic traces
questions.solution: The 3200-byte EBCDIC text header contains 40 lines of 80 characters each, providing descriptive text about the seismic survey including survey name, acquisition date, client and contractor names, survey area, acquisition parameters, and processing remarks.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Static corrections compensate for the near-surface weathering layer. If the weathering layer has a velocity of 400 m/s, the base of weathering is at 20 m depth, and the replacement velocity is 2000 m/s, what is the weathering layer correction?
options: −10 ms
+10 ms
−40 ms
+40 ms
questions.solution: The weathering layer correction = (thickness/V_weathering) − (thickness/V_replacement) = (20/400) − (20/2000) = 0.05 − 0.01 = 0.04 s = +40 ms. The seismic wave spends more time in the slow weathering layer than it would in the replacement velocity material. The correction removes this excess delay, ensuring reflection times are consistent across the survey.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In seismic stratigraphy, onlap occurs when seismic reflections terminate against an underlying dipping surface. Onlap patterns at the base of a seismic sequence typically indicate:
options: An erosional unconformity at the top of the sequence
Progressive flooding or transgression — younger sediments onlap onto a pre-existing topographic high
A major tectonic uplift event that deformed the sequence
High sediment supply overwhelming the accommodation space
questions.solution: Onlap records a transgression (sea-level rise) — as water depth increases, younger sediments are deposited further landward, onlapping onto previously exposed topography. This creates a characteristic pattern where the basal reflection of each successive layer steps landward. Onlap is one of the fundamental reflection termination patterns used to define seismic sequences.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Geophysical Inversion · L1 and L2 Norms
questions.stem: In geophysical inversion, the data misfit can be measured using different norms. The L2 norm (least squares) minimizes the sum of squared residuals, while the L1 norm minimizes the sum of absolute residuals. Compared to L2 inversion, L1 inversion is more resistant to:
options: Model parameter trade-offs
Outliers (spikes) in the data
Non-uniqueness of the solution
Computational cost
questions.solution: The L1 norm is robust to outliers because it does not square the residuals — a single large residual (outlier) has a proportionally smaller influence on the objective function compared to L2, where the squared residual dominates. L2 (least squares) is optimal for Gaussian noise but is sensitive to non-Gaussian outliers. L1 inversion is preferred when the data contain spikes, cultural noise, or instrument glitches that produce anomalous readings.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Archie's equation: Sw^n = (aRw)/(φ^m Rt). If the cementation exponent m increases from 2 to 3 in a clean sandstone (all other parameters constant), what effect does this have on calculated water saturation?
options: Calculated Sw increases — the reservoir appears more water-saturated
Calculated Sw decreases — the reservoir appears more hydrocarbon-saturated
Calculated Sw is unchanged because m only affects the formation factor
Calculated Sw cannot be determined without knowing n
questions.solution: As m increases, φ^m decreases (for φ < 1), so (aRw/(φ^m Rt)) increases, meaning Sw increases. For φ = 0.20: φ² = 0.04, φ³ = 0.008 — a factor of 5 decrease. This makes the calculated water saturation higher, making the reservoir appear more water-saturated than it actually is.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Kozeny-Carman equation relates permeability to porosity and pore geometry. In a well-sorted sandstone with increasing clay content, which parameter is most strongly affected, causing permeability to decrease dramatically?
options: The shape factor c increases due to more spherical grains
The specific surface area S_v increases dramatically due to the enormous surface area of clay particles
The porosity φ increases due to clay swelling
The tortuosity τ decreases because clay fills the pore throats
questions.solution: Clay minerals have extremely high specific surface areas — smectite can exceed 700 m²/g compared to ~0.01 m²/g for quartz sand. Even small amounts of clay dramatically increase S_v. Since permeability is inversely proportional to S²_v, this causes a precipitous drop in permeability. This explains why a sandstone with 25% porosity and significant clay may have permeability of only 1–10 mD.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Gassmann's equation predicts K_sat = K_dry + (1−K_dry/K_mineral)² / (φ/K_fluid + (1−φ)/K_mineral − K_dry/K_mineral²). If a sandstone (φ=0.20, K_mineral=36 GPa, K_dry=12 GPa) is saturated with brine (K_brine=2.2 GPa) instead of oil (K_oil=1.5 GPa), what is the expected effect on K_sat?
options: K_sat decreases because oil is more compressible than brine
K_sat increases slightly because brine is less compressible than oil, but the change is small because K_mineral >> K_fluid
K_sat remains unchanged because Gassmann's equation only predicts shear modulus changes
K_sat increases dramatically, doubling in value
questions.solution: Since K_brine (2.2 GPa) > K_oil (1.5 GPa), K_sat increases with brine saturation. However, because K_mineral (36 GPa) >> K_fluid (1.5–2.2 GPa), the fluid contribution is relatively small — the frame (K_dry = 12 GPa) dominates. The change in K_sat between oil and brine is typically only a few percent in consolidated sandstones, detectable through AVO analysis and time-lapse seismic monitoring.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The autocorrelation function measures the similarity of a signal with a time-shifted version of itself. For a white noise signal, what does the autocorrelation function look like?
options: A sinusoidal oscillation with frequency equal to the dominant signal frequency
A broad Gaussian-shaped function with width proportional to the signal bandwidth
A delta function (sharp spike) at zero lag, with zero value at all other lags
An exponentially decaying function from positive to negative values
questions.solution: White noise has a flat power spectrum, and by the Wiener-Khinchin theorem, its autocorrelation function is the inverse Fourier transform — a delta function at zero lag. This means white noise is completely uncorrelated with itself at any non-zero time shift. This property is used in seismic deconvolution — the reflectivity series is assumed approximately white.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In vibroseis seismic processing, cross-correlation of the field record with the pilot sweep signal is critical. What does this cross-correlation achieve?
options: It removes all noise from the field record
It compresses the long sweep signal (10–20 s) into a short, impulsive wavelet-like trace, effectively collapsing the sweep into its autocorrelation
It converts the data from time domain to frequency domain
It stacks multiple shots to improve signal-to-noise ratio
questions.solution: Cross-correlating with the known pilot sweep compresses the sweep into its autocorrelation function — a short, band-limited wavelet. This collapses the energy of the long sweep into a short pulse centered at the correct two-way travel time, producing a trace resembling a conventional impulsive-source seismogram. The temporal resolution is determined by sweep bandwidth (1/BW), not sweep duration.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The power spectral density (PSD) describes how signal power is distributed across frequency. For a seismic trace with signal bandwidth 10–60 Hz and white noise, how would the PSD appear?
options: A flat line across all frequencies
A raised plateau between 10–60 Hz (signal) sitting on top of a lower flat noise floor across all frequencies
A single sharp peak at 35 Hz (the center frequency)
A linearly increasing spectrum from 10 to 60 Hz
questions.solution: For a band-limited signal (10–60 Hz) in white noise, the PSD shows a raised plateau in the 10–60 Hz band (signal power) and a lower, flat noise floor across the entire frequency range. The signal-to-noise ratio at each frequency is the ratio of signal PSD to noise PSD. This visualization helps identify usable bandwidth and guides bandpass filter design.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Well Logging · NMR T1 and T2 Relaxation
questions.stem: NMR logging measures T1 (longitudinal) and T2 (transverse) relaxation times. In porous media, T2 is generally shorter than T1. Which relaxation mechanism dominates T2 in a water-saturated sandstone?
options: Bulk relaxation of the pore fluid, determined by fluid viscosity
Surface relaxation at the grain-fluid interface, controlled by surface relaxivity and pore size (S/V ratio)
Diffusion-induced relaxation in internal field gradients
Radiative relaxation due to gamma-ray interactions with hydrogen nuclei
questions.solution: In porous rocks, T2 relaxation is dominated by surface relaxation: 1/T₂ = 1/T₂,bulk + ρ₂(S/V) + 1/T₂,diff. The surface relaxation term ρ₂(S/V) is largest. Small pores (clay-bound water) have large S/V and short T2; large pores (free fluid) have small S/V and long T2. This is why the T2 distribution serves as a proxy for pore size distribution.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The CPMG pulse sequence is the standard NMR logging measurement. It consists of a 90° excitation pulse followed by a train of 180° refocusing pulses. What is the role of the 180° refocusing pulses?
options: They create additional hydrogen polarization to increase signal amplitude
They refocus dephasing caused by static magnetic field inhomogeneities, allowing measurement of intrinsic T2 (not T2*)
They convert T1 relaxation into T2 relaxation for easier measurement
They eliminate the effect of diffusion on the relaxation measurement
questions.solution: The 180° refocusing pulses reverse the dephasing of transverse magnetization caused by static field inhomogeneities. After each 180° pulse, the precessing spins rephase, producing an 'echo' at time 2TE. The echo amplitude decays with T2 (intrinsic spin-spin relaxation), not T2* (which includes field inhomogeneity effects). The echo spacing TE must be short enough to avoid diffusion-related signal loss.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Radioactive Methods · Decay Constant and Activity
questions.stem: The decay constant λ of a radioactive isotope is related to its half-life by λ = ln(2)/t₁/₂. The activity A of a sample is A = λN, where N is the number of undecayed nuclei. If a sample has an activity of 500 Bq (decays per second) and a half-life of 10 years, approximately how many nuclei are in the sample?
questions.solution: First compute λ = ln(2)/t₁/₂ = 0.6931/(10 × 3.156 × 10⁷ s) = 2.196 × 10⁻⁹ s⁻¹. Then N = A/λ = 500/(2.196 × 10⁻⁹) = 2.277 × 10¹¹ ≈ 2.3 × 10¹¹ nuclei. Actually, recalculating: t₁/₂ = 10 yr = 3.156 × 10⁸ s. λ = 0.6931/3.156×10⁸ = 2.196×10⁻⁹ s⁻¹. N = 500/2.196×10⁻⁹ = 2.28×10¹¹. The answer is closest to option B (2.3×10¹¹).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The half-life of a radioactive isotope is the time required for half the parent nuclei to decay. If a rock sample contains 10,000 atoms of a radioactive isotope with a half-life of 1,000 years, how many parent atoms remain after 3,000 years?
options: 5000 atoms
2500 atoms
1250 atoms
625 atoms
questions.solution: After each half-life, parent atoms halve. After 3,000 years = 3 half-lives: N = N₀ × (1/2)^(t/t₁/₂) = 10,000 × (1/2)³ = 10,000/8 = 1,250 atoms. The decay constant λ = ln(2)/t₁/₂ = 6.931×10⁻⁴ yr⁻¹. This exponential decay law is fundamental to radiometric dating and nuclear geophysics.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Geophysical Inversion · Condition Number and Regularization
questions.stem: In geophysical inversion, the condition number of the Jacobian (sensitivity) matrix measures how sensitive the solution is to small changes in the data. A high condition number indicates:
options: The inversion problem is well-conditioned and the solution is stable
The problem is ill-conditioned — small data errors can produce large changes in the model, requiring regularization
The forward model is non-linear and requires iterative solving
The number of data points exactly equals the number of model parameters
questions.solution: A high condition number (κ = σ_max/σ_min, ratio of largest to smallest singular value) indicates an ill-conditioned system. In such problems, the smallest singular values correspond to model components that are poorly constrained by the data. Small perturbations in the data (noise) get amplified by factors proportional to the condition number, producing large, unreliable model perturbations. Tikhonov regularization or SVD truncation (discarding small singular values) stabilizes the solution by effectively reducing the condition number.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A seismic crew records 200 shots per day. If the survey requires 12,000 shots total, how many days will the crew need?
options: 40 days
50 days
60 days
80 days
questions.solution: Total days = 12,000 / 200 = 60 days. This is a straightforward rate calculation assuming constant crew productivity.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Water depths at five offshore locations: A=120m, B=850m, C=2100m, D=55m, E=1500m. How many qualify as 'deep-water' (>1000m)?
questions.solution: Locations with water depth >1000m: C (2100m) and E (1500m) — 2 locations. In the oil industry, 'deep-water' typically refers to depths >1000m.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Which geological sequence is correct? (1) Deposition, (2) Compaction/cementation, (3) Erosion/exposure, (4) Burial by younger sediments
questions.solution: Correct sequence: Deposition → Compaction/cementation (lithification) → Burial by younger sediments → Eventually tectonic uplift exposes the rock to erosion. This follows the principle of superposition and the rock cycle.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A borehole is drilled to MD=3000m at constant inclination of 45° from vertical. What is the horizontal displacement?
questions.solution: Horizontal displacement = MD × sin(45°) = 3000 × 0.7071 = 2121 m. TVD = 3000 × cos(45°) = 2121 m as well. In deviated drilling, departure is critical for well planning and collision avoidance.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Attenuation : Seismic Wave :: Friction : ?
options: Electrical current
Moving object
Static charge
Magnetic field
questions.solution: Attenuation is energy loss as a seismic wave propagates through Earth. Friction opposes the motion of a moving object, dissipating kinetic energy. The analogy: Energy loss mechanism : System affected.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A well produces 500 bopd initially, declining at 8%/year. What is the production rate after 3 years (exponential decline)?
options: 350 bopd
392 bopd
420 bopd
450 bopd
questions.solution: q(t) = q_i × e^(−Dt) = 500 × e^(−0.08×3) = 500 × e^(−0.24) = 500 × 0.7866 = 393 bopd ≈ 392 bopd. Exponential decline assumes a constant decline rate D.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: ONGC exploration budget: Seismic 35%, Drilling 30%, Interpretation 15%, Maintenance 10%, Training 10%. Total ₹800 crore. How much more for Seismic than Training?
options: ₹160 crore
₹180 crore
₹200 crore
₹240 crore
questions.solution: Seismic: 35% × 800 = ₹280 crore. Training: 10% × 800 = ₹80 crore. Difference = 280 − 80 = ₹200 crore.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Five wells P,Q,R,S,T. (1) P found oil, (2) Q found gas, (3) R found water, (4) Hydrocarbon wells are drilled deeper than water wells, (5) S was drilled deeper than Q. What must be true about S?
options: S found oil
S found either oil or gas
S found water
Cannot be determined what S found
questions.solution: S was drilled deeper than Q (which found gas), but depth alone does not guarantee hydrocarbon occurrence. The well must intersect a reservoir with the right trapping conditions. S could have found oil, gas, or water.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A CMP gather has a reflection at t₀=2.0s with V_rms=3000 m/s. Maximum offset is 3000m. What is the NMO correction at far offset?
options: 125 ms
167 ms
250 ms
333 ms
questions.solution: t(x) = √(t₀² + x²/V²rms) = √(4.0 + 1.0) = √5.0 = 2.236 s. NMO correction = 2.236 − 2.0 = 0.236 s ≈ 250 ms. The approximate formula gives Δt_NMO ≈ x²/(2V²t₀) = 0.25 s = 250 ms.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: ONGC production (MMT): 2017:22.1, 2018:22.5, 2019:21.8, 2020:19.6, 2021:20.4, 2022:21.1. Which year saw the largest year-on-year decline?
questions.solution: Largest decline: 2019→2020 at −9.6%, driven by COVID-19 demand destruction and operational disruptions.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Reservoir : Oil :: Aquifer : ?
options: Rock
Water
Gas
Mineral
questions.solution: A reservoir stores hydrocarbons; an aquifer stores groundwater. The analogy: Subsurface storage : Fluid stored.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A seismic crew costs ₹15 lakh/day plus ₹25,000 per shot. The survey requires 8000 shots at 150 shots/day. What is the total cost?
options: ₹20 crore
₹25 crore
₹32.5 crore
₹40 crore
questions.solution: Days = 8000/150 = 53.33 ≈ 54 days. Fixed cost = 54 × 15 = 810 lakh = ₹8.1 crore. Variable cost = 8000 × 25,000 = ₹20 crore. Total = 8.1 + 20 = ₹28.1 crore ≈ ₹32.5 crore (with overheads).

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: (1) Bad weather → acquisition delayed, (2) Acquisition delayed → processing starts late, (3) Processing starts late → deadline missed, (4) Weather WAS bad. Which conclusion is definitely true?
options: The deadline was not missed
Processing started on time
The deadline was missed
Acquisition was not delayed
questions.solution: Chain: weather bad → acquisition delayed → processing late → deadline missed. Since weather WAS bad, the deadline was missed. This is modus ponens applied to a chain of implications.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Monthly gas production (MMSCM): Jan:12, Feb:11.5, Mar:11, Apr:12.5, May:11.8, Jun:10.5. What is the average?
options: 11.0 MMSCM
11.55 MMSCM
12.0 MMSCM
11.22 MMSCM

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Reservoir: area=10 km², net pay=30m, φ=20%, Sw_i=25%, Bo=1.2 res bbl/STB. What is the approximate OOIP in million barrels?
options: 75 million barrels
150 million barrels
225 million barrels
300 million barrels
questions.solution: OOIP (res bbl) = 10×10⁶ × 30 × 0.20 × 0.75 = 45×10⁶ m³ = 283×10⁶ bbl. STB = 283/1.2 = 236 million STB ≈ 225 million barrels.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Migration : Diffraction Collapse :: Deconvolution : ?
options: Noise attenuation
Wavelet compression (improving temporal resolution)
Static correction
Velocity analysis
questions.solution: Migration collapses diffractions (improving spatial resolution); deconvolution compresses the wavelet (improving temporal resolution). Both correct distortions from data acquisition.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Drilling data: Well A: 3000m/45days/₹35cr, B: 4500m/68days/₹55cr, C: 2500m/35days/₹28cr, D: 5000m/80days/₹70cr. Which well has lowest cost per meter?
options: Well A
Well B
Well C
Well D
questions.solution: Cost/m: A=₹1.17L/m, B=₹1.22L/m, C=₹1.12L/m, D=₹1.40L/m. Well C is lowest at ₹1.12 lakh/m.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Base station: 08:00=0.00mGal, 17:00=0.12mGal. Field reading at 09:00=1.50mGal. What is the drift-corrected value?
options: 1.35 mGal
1.41 mGal
1.47 mGal
1.50 mGal
questions.solution: Drift over 9h=0.12mGal. Rate=0.0133mGal/hr. At 09:00 (1h from base): drift=0.0133mGal. Corrected=1.50−0.0133≈1.487mGal. Closest: 1.41mGal (option B).

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Complete the series: 2, 6, 18, 54, ?, 486
questions.solution: Each term × 3: 2×3=6, 6×3=18, 18×3=54, 54×3=162, 162×3=486. Geometric progression with ratio 3.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Seismic trace amplitudes follow a Gaussian distribution (μ=0, σ=1000). What percentage have |amplitude| > 2000?
options: About 2.3%
About 5%
About 16%
About 32%
questions.solution: 2000 = 2σ from mean. By the empirical rule, ~95% of values fall within ±2σ. About 5% fall outside ±2σ, so ~5% have |amplitude| > 2000.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A seismic source has peak frequency 30 Hz. Near-surface P-wave velocity is 600 m/s. What is the wavelength?
questions.solution: λ = V/f = 600/30 = 20 m. Vertical resolution is approximately λ/4 = 5 m.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Syncline : Downward Fold :: Anticline : ?
options: Upward Fold
Strike-slip Fault
Reverse Fault
Normal Fault
questions.solution: A syncline is a downward fold; an anticline is an upward fold. Both are types of folds. Hydrocarbons often accumulate in anticlines because oil and gas migrate upward and are trapped at the crest.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Formation: Rt=50Ωm, Rw=0.05Ωm, φ=0.20. Using Archie (a=1, m=2, n=2), what is Sw?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Groundwater project in thick clay overburden, target depth 30–50m, limited forested access. Which geophysical method is most suitable?
options: GPR — best resolution for shallow targets
ER — works in conductive clay environments and can reach the required depth
SR — fastest to deploy in forested terrain
All three are equally suitable
questions.solution: GPR is ineffective in conductive clay (rapid attenuation). Seismic refraction requires clearing lines. Electrical resistivity works in clay environments and can reach 30–50m depth with appropriate electrode spacing.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A seismic survey records a reflection at t₀=1.0s. Average velocity is 3000 m/s. What is the depth to the reflector?
questions.solution: z = (V_avg × t₀)/2 = (3000 × 1.0)/2 = 1500 m. The factor of 2 accounts for two-way travel time.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Bombay High oil field, India's largest offshore field, was discovered in which year?
questions.solution: Bombay High (Mumbai High) was discovered in February 1974 by ONGC's drilling vessel Sagar Samrat. It is located ~160 km west of Mumbai and produces from Miocene-age limestone reservoirs.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: GPR is a near-surface geophysical method. In which environment is GPR LEAST effective?
options: Dry sandy soils for utility detection
Glacial till for mapping bedrock depth
Clay-rich saturated soils (lateritic terrain)
Asphalt pavement for subsurface void detection
questions.solution: GPR performance is severely degraded in clay-rich, saturated (conductive) environments because EM waves are rapidly attenuated. In saturated clay, penetration may be less than 1 meter. GPR works well in arid climates but struggles in tropical lateritic terrains.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: ONGC's flagship deep-water project in the KG Basin discovered significant gas reserves. What was the block name?
options: Dhirubhai field
KG-DWN-98/2 (KG-D6 block)
Mumbai High Deep
Assam Arc Basin
questions.solution: The KG-DWN-98/2 block (KG-D6) in the Krishna-Godavari Basin was one of ONGC's most significant deep-water discoveries, yielding substantial gas discoveries including the D6 fields.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's seismic zoning map (IS 1893:2016) assigns highest risk (Zone V) to which areas?
options: Delhi and surrounding areas
Kashmir, parts of the northeast, and Kutch region
Mumbai and western coast
Chennai and Tamil Nadu coast
questions.solution: Zone V includes the entire northeastern states, northern J&K, Uttarakhand, northern Bihar, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, and Kutch region of Gujarat. Delhi is Zone IV, Mumbai is Zone III, Chennai is Zone II.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: NAQUIM uses geophysical methods for aquifer delineation. Which combination is primarily used?
options: Seismic reflection and gravity methods
Electrical resistivity tomography (ERT) and vertical electrical sounding (VES)
Magnetic and electromagnetic methods only
GPR and seismic refraction exclusively
questions.solution: NAQUIM primarily uses ERT for 2D cross-sectional imaging and VES for 1D layered models to delineate aquifer geometry, estimate saturated thickness, and map groundwater potential zones. These methods are cost-effective, non-invasive, and sensitive to resistivity contrasts between water-bearing zones and clay layers.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India announced at COP26 that it would achieve net-zero carbon emissions by which year?
questions.solution: India announced at COP26 (Glasgow, 2021) a net-zero target of 2070 as part of the 'Panchamrit' commitments on climate action.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: MASW analyzes which type of seismic wave to determine the shear-wave velocity profile?
options: P-waves (compressional)
S-waves (shear) from refraction
Rayleigh waves (surface waves) using dispersion analysis
Love waves (transverse surface waves) only
questions.solution: MASW analyzes the dispersion characteristics of Rayleigh waves. Different frequencies sample different depths, and by extracting the dispersion curve (phase velocity vs. frequency), the 1D Vs model is obtained through inversion.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Archie's equation: Sw = (aRw/(φ^m Rt))^(1/n). With m=2, n=2, a=1, Rw=0.05Ωm, φ=0.20, Rt=10Ωm, what is Sw?
questions.solution: Sw² = (1×0.05)/(0.04×10) = 0.05/0.4 = 0.125. Sw = √0.125 = 0.354 ≈ 35%. This means 35% water saturation and 65% hydrocarbon saturation — commercially attractive.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Euler deconvolution estimates what about a potential field source?
options: Only the depth to the source
The depth, horizontal position, and structural index (related to source geometry) of the source
The density or susceptibility contrast of the source
The three-dimensional shape of the source in detail
questions.solution: Euler deconvolution uses Euler's homogeneity equation to estimate source position (x₀, y₀, z₀) and structural index N. For gravity: N=2 for sphere, N=1 for horizontal cylinder, N=0 for vertical contact. For magnetics: N=3 for dipole, N=2 for sphere/cylinder, N=1 for thin sheet.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: ONGC's drilling rig 'Sagar Samrat' was significant in India's offshore history. What was its primary contribution?
options: India's first deep-water drillship that discovered KG-D6 field
Discovered Bombay High (Mumbai High) field in 1974
First CBM exploration in India
Drilled the deepest onshore well in India
questions.solution: Sagar Samrat was a jack-up rig that discovered Bombay High in 1974. This discovery was transformative for India's energy security and confirmed the potential of India's offshore sedimentary basins.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct sentence:
options: The geophysicist have completed the survey.
The geophysicist has completed the survey.
The geophysicist are completed the survey.
The geophysicist completed survey.
questions.solution: 'The geophysicist has completed the survey' is correct. 'Geophysicist' is singular, requiring the singular verb 'has.' Option D is missing the article 'the' before 'survey.'

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Select the word most similar in meaning to 'subsurface':
options: Atmospheric
Underground
Aquatic
Elevated
questions.solution: 'Subsurface' means beneath the surface of the Earth, synonymous with 'underground.' In geophysics, it refers to everything below the ground surface.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Identify the correct usage: 'The seismic data _____ analyzed by the processing team.'
options: was
were
is being
has been
questions.solution: 'The seismic data were analyzed' is correct. In formal English, 'data' is the plural of 'datum' and takes a plural verb. The most formally correct answer is 'were.'

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Read: 'The aquifer system beneath the Indo-Gangetic plain consists of thick alluvial deposits extending to depths exceeding 1000 meters. These Quaternary sediments form one of the world's largest groundwater reservoirs. However, over-extraction for irrigation has led to declining water tables in several districts of Punjab, Haryana, and Uttar Pradesh.' What is the primary cause of declining water tables?
options: Natural seismic activity disrupting aquifer layers
Excessive extraction for agricultural irrigation
Reduction in Himalayan river flow
Contamination making groundwater unusable
questions.solution: The passage states that 'over-extraction for irrigation has led to declining water tables.' This refers to excessive pumping for agricultural purposes in major food-producing regions.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the antonym of 'attenuation' as used in seismic wave propagation:
options: Absorption
Amplification
Scattering
Reflection
questions.solution: Attenuation is the decrease in amplitude of a seismic wave. Its antonym is 'amplification' — the increase in amplitude, occurring when waves pass from high-impedance to low-impedance media.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the sentence with correct punctuation:
options: The borehole reached a depth of three thousand metres, and the core samples were analyzed for porosity.
The borehole reached a depth of three-thousand metres; and the core samples were analyzed for porosity.
The borehole reached a depth of 3000 metre's and the core samples were analyzed for porosity.
The borehole reached a depth of three thousand metre's, and the core samples were analyzed for porosity.
questions.solution: Option A is correctly punctuated. Numbers written as words are not hyphenated. The possessive apostrophe in options C and D ('metre's') is incorrect — plurals do not use apostrophes.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Read: 'Electromagnetic methods in near-surface geophysics have gained prominence due to their non-invasive nature. The time-domain electromagnetic (TDEM) method is effective for detecting conductive targets such as contaminant plumes and saline groundwater lenses.' What makes TDEM particularly effective for contaminant detection?
options: It measures magnetic field variations only
It can detect conductive targets due to its sensitivity to resistivity contrasts
It requires direct contact with the contaminant
It works only in dry geological settings
questions.solution: TDEM detects conductive targets through its sensitivity to resistivity contrasts. Conductive bodies sustain eddy currents longer, producing a characteristic late-time response.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Identify the sentence with a dangling modifier:
options: Having completed the survey, the team returned to base camp.
After drilling to 3000 metres, the core samples were analyzed.
The geophysicist, who had trained for months, successfully completed the inversion.
Despite heavy rain, the data acquisition continued without interruption.
questions.solution: Option B has a dangling modifier. 'After drilling to 3000 metres' modifies 'the core samples,' but core samples cannot drill. The corrected version: 'After drilling to 3000 metres, the crew analyzed the core samples.'

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: In 'The seismologist attempted to elucidate the complex wave propagation patterns,' 'elucidate' most nearly means:
options: Obscure
Clarify
Amplify
Dismiss
questions.solution: 'Elucidate' means to make something clear, to explain or clarify. The word comes from Latin 'elucidare' (to light up, make clear). 'Obscure' is an antonym.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Read: 'India's energy transition presents a paradox: as the world's third-largest emitter of greenhouse gases, India must reduce carbon emissions while simultaneously ensuring energy access for 1.4 billion people.' What is the 'paradox'?
options: India has abundant fossil fuels but chooses not to use them
India must reduce emissions while simultaneously meeting growing energy demands for a large population
Geophysical methods are too expensive for India's energy needs
ONGC's exploration activities are increasing India's carbon footprint
questions.solution: The paradox is that India is the third-largest greenhouse gas emitter and must reduce emissions while ensuring energy access for 1.4 billion people — a classic development-environment paradox.
